key_facts: Eskilian was born in Soviet Armenia and immigrated to the United States in 1978.
In 2011, Eskilian was ordered removed because of a grand theft conviction.
Armenia would not accept individuals born during the Soviet era, so Eskilian was deemed 'stateless' and not deported.
In June 2018, Eskilian learned she could be removed to Armenia and quickly obtained counsel.
Counsel successfully had Eskilian's conviction vacated on May 24, 2019.
Eskilian filed a motion to reopen and dismiss removal proceedings based on the vacated conviction in July 2019.
The IJ denied the motion, finding no due diligence in seeking vacatur of the conviction.
Eskilian filed a second motion to reopen based on ineffective assistance of counsel, but the IJ denied it as number-barred.
The BIA dismissed the second motion, finding no due diligence and strict Lozada compliance required.
legal_issues: The diligence standard used by the BIA in upholding the IJ's denial of Eskilian's second motion to reopen.
Whether Eskilian acted with due diligence in pursuing vacatur of her conviction.
decision: The panel granted Susie Eskilian’s petition for review, vacated the BIA’s decision, and remanded, holding that the BIA employed the wrong diligence standard and that Eskilian acted with due diligence in pursuing vacatur of her conviction.
reasoning_summary: The panel held that for individuals who were born during the Soviet era or who are otherwise deemed 'stateless,' it is reasonable to expect that an individual will begin to take action to challenge removability only once that individual learns that they can be removed to a country and thus are no longer 'stateless.' The panel found that Eskilian acted with due diligence because she became aware in June 2018 that she could be removed, obtained counsel in August 2018, had her conviction vacated in May 2019, and moved to reopen in July 2019.
